title: 3 Year Old Unpredictably Rough, Aggressive
recap: The articles talks about key issues: the fight-or-flight affect gap and the normal-laughter signal. It explains them as: children lashing out when overstimulated or transitioning, where a lack of visible anger suggests emotional suppression and spaciness indicates a freeze response. Proposing solutions: playful rough-housing to trigger giggles, identifying personal space bubbles, and avoiding forced physical contact. Explaining why they work: laughter vents the same anxieties as tears; naming personal boundaries empowers verbal over physical self-defense; and parents learning to read the unpredictable early warning signs allows for intervention before dysregulation peaks.

title: 4 Year Old - Aggressive Tantrums, Screaming
recap: The articles talks about key issues: locked-up fears and the venting release. It explains them as: surface anger masking deeper terrors that require a safe container for discharge, where traditional redirection may inadvertently increase frustration. Proposing solutions: therapeutic scary monster games where the child wins, staying physically close with minimal words during meltdowns, and validating the magnitude of the upset. Explaining why they work: laughter releases the same biological tension as tears; calm acceptance creates the safety for fear to surface; and successful emotional release leads to a naturally relaxed and cooperative state.

title: 5 year old explosive temper, hitting
recap: The articles talks about key issues: the fragility of self-worth and the perfectionism defense. It explains them as: a child equating performance with goodness, where any struggle feels like a threatening attack on the self that must be met with physical aggression. Proposing solutions: establishing Nobody is perfect as a family mantra, going under anger to validate vulnerable feelings, and providing squeezey ball alternatives. Explaining why they work: self-acceptance reduces the need for defensive attacks; naming sensations builds reflective relationship with emotions; and being accepted during messy feelings builds durable internal happiness.

title: 6 Year Old with Explosive Temper
recap: The articles talks about key issues: anger as a defense and the learning delay in changing gears. It explains them as: some children having neurological difficulty with emotional flexibility, while others use rage to protect against vulnerable feelings of sibling rivalry or school insecurity. Proposing solutions: offering Chill Time (not time-out) for snuggling and deep breaths, 30 minutes of unstructured one-on-one time, and healing parental disapproval through visualization. Explaining why they work: co-regulation proves big feelings are survivable; play-acting sister conflicts provides emotional distance; and parental self-regulation prevents the bullying model of punishment-based discipline.

title: When Children Hit Themselves or Call Themselves Names
recap: The articles talks about key issues: the perfectionism gap and internalized punishment. It explains them as: three-year-olds hitting themselves as a physical redirection of frustration when their vision exceeds their skills, often reflecting an internalized harsh discipline style. Proposing solutions: modeling self-compassion aloud, providing hope-based language ("you're getting close"), and teaching deep breathing to handle the "frustration-fire." Explaining why they work: validating emotions removes the sense of isolation; normalizing struggle developmentally prevents a negative self-identity; and shifting from punishment to positive discipline stops the child from treating themselves as a "naughty person" who deserves pain.

title: 5 year old Aggressive Tantrums
recap: The articles talks about key issues: the I am NOT sad defense and the reconnection attack. It explains them as: children attacking parents when they feel disconnected, using anger to mask deeper fears they cannot yet verbalize. Proposing solutions: acknowledging the specific disappointment immediately, protecting oneself without leaving the room, and daily laughter-prevention. Explaining why they work: rage only dissipates when fully heard; staying close prevents the added trauma of isolation; and sobbing (the goal of the session) releases the fear that was driving the aggression.
